---
title: Plugins
---

*Calepin* produces clean HTML, LaTeX, Typst, and Markdown out of the box, but every project has unique needs: custom div styles, new Jinja functions, branded page templates, or entirely new output formats. Plugins let you extend *Calepin* without modifying its source.

A plugin can provide any combination of:

- **Filters** --- transform fenced divs (`::: {.myclass} ... :::`) and bracketed spans (`[text]{.myclass}`) into custom output.
- **Custom functions** --- register Jinja functions callable as {% raw %}`{{ name(args) }}`{% endraw %} in document bodies.
- **Postprocessors** --- rewrite the entire rendered document (e.g., split HTML into slides).
- **Templates** --- override element or page templates to change how divs, figures, or the full page are rendered.
- **Custom formats** --- define new output formats built on top of HTML, LaTeX, Typst, or Markdown.

Plugins are written as executable scripts---Python, R, shell, Ruby, Node, or any language that reads JSON from stdin and writes to stdout. No compilation step, no special SDK.


# Plugin structure

A plugin is a directory with a `plugin.yml` manifest at its root. Here is a typical layout:

```
_calepin/plugins/accordion/
  plugin.yml          # required: declares capabilities
  filter.py           # executable for the filter hook
```

A more full-featured plugin might look like:

```
_calepin/plugins/mytheme/
  plugin.yml
  filter.py           # filter logic
  shortcode.py        # custom function logic
  postprocess.py      # document postprocessor
  elements/           # element template overrides
    theorem.html
    callout-note.html
  templates/          # page template overrides
    calepin.html
    calepin.css
  style.csl           # citation style
```


# The manifest: `plugin.yml`

Every field under `provides` is optional. A plugin declares only what it needs.

```yaml
name: accordion
version: 0.1.0
description: "Collapsible accordion sections"

provides:
  # A single filter
  filter:
    run: filter.py
    match:
      classes: [accordion]
    contexts: [div]

  # Or multiple filters with different match rules and scripts
  filters:
    - run: accordion.py
      match:
        classes: [accordion]
      contexts: [div]
    - run: tooltip.py
      match:
        classes: [tooltip]
      contexts: [span]
      formats: [html]

  shortcode:
    run: shortcode.py
    names: [greeting, farewell]    # function names to register

  postprocess:
    run: postprocess.py
    formats: [html]                # output formats (omit = all)

  elements:
    dir: elements/                 # {name}.{format} template files

  templates:
    dir: templates/                # calepin.{format}, calepin.css

  csl: style.csl                   # citation style file

  format:
    name: myformat                 # custom output format
    base: html
    extension: html
    preprocess: scripts/pre.sh
    postprocess: scripts/post.sh
```

All paths are relative to the plugin directory. Match fields under `match` are OR'd: a filter triggers when *any* rule matches.

Filters can be declared as singular (`filter:` with one object) or plural (`filters:` with a list). A plugin can define multiple filters, each with its own executable, match rules, and contexts. Both forms can coexist in the same manifest.

Example plugins are available in the [plugins/ directory on GitHub](https://github.com/vincentarelbundock/calepin/tree/main/plugins).


# Using plugins

1. Place the plugin directory in `_calepin/plugins/` (project).
2. List it in front matter:

```yaml
calepin:
  plugins:
    - accordion
    - mytheme
```

Plugins are dispatched in the order listed. Built-in plugins (tabset, layout, figure-div, theorem, callout) run after user plugins. The first plugin to handle an element wins.

To scaffold a new plugin, create a directory in `_calepin/plugins/` with a `plugin.yml` manifest. See the [plugin structure](#plugin-structure) section above for the expected layout.


# Built-in plugins

These are compiled into *Calepin* and always active. A user plugin can override any of them by declaring the same match rules.

| Plugin | Matches | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `tabset` | `.panel-tabset` class | Tabbed panels |
| `layout` | `layout-ncol`, `layout-nrow`, `layout` attrs | Grid layouts |
| `figure-div` | `fig-` id prefix | Figure divs with captions |
| `table-div` | `tbl-` id prefix | Table divs with captions |
| `theorem` | `.theorem`, `.lemma`, `.corollary`, etc. | Auto-numbered theorem environments |
| `callout` | `.callout-note`, `.callout-tip`, etc. | Callout boxes with icons |


# What's next

The following pages cover each plugin capability in detail:

- [Shortcuts](../templates/shortcuts.qmd) --- built-in functions, context variables, and plugin functions.
- [Filters](filters.qmd) --- writing filter plugins for divs and spans.
- [Templates](../templates/elements.qmd) --- element and page templates, and template plugins.
